reason: The item cleanly tests boundary-aware inference from interstitial thermocouple/thermography observations to propagation mechanisms, rewarding restraint about what each measurement can and cannot imply. Decoys are engineering-plausible and mostly single-fault, targeting common overextensions (internal reaction inference, thermodynamic “guarantee,” and component-specific temperature claims). Discrimination is strong because selecting all and only B/C/F requires integrating time-profile interpretation with geometric heat-flux limitations while rejecting tempting but unsupported escalations.

The item cleanly targets boundary-aware engineering judgment: whether a single non-propagation event warrants removing active thermal management, and the correct options consistently enforce scope limits (abuse-mode specificity, sufficiency vs contribution, and generalization constraints). Decoys are plausible and mostly single-fault, but two incorrect choices (A, F) lean on the same universal-safety overgeneralization, slightly reducing discriminative diversity and increasing the chance of testwise elimination rather than deep reasoning.

The item cleanly tests a single boundary-aware judgment: whether an interstitial thermocouple plateau can be interpreted as internal thermal-runaway arrest versus being an external transport/venting artifact. Correct options consistently hinge on measurement-location limits, and most decoys are plausible but commit identifiable inference overreach. Discrimination is strong for experts, though two decoys lean toward similar “mechanism speculation from external data” errors, slightly reducing single-fault purity and separation.

The prompt tightly anchors the evaluation to what a single non-propagation observation can legitimately establish versus what it cannot, directly probing boundary-aware engineering admissibility. It elicits a mechanistic identification (contact-area-limited conduction) while forcing explicit limits on generalization and design inference, creating good expert/novice separation. Some scoring headroom is lost because this is a short-answer item (no explicit decoys to audit) and because wording like “inherently safe” can invite slightly subjective rhetoric rather than strictly structured evidentiary logic.
